region_of_origin: Portugal / Southern Africa (Mozambique / South Africa); served internationally

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries food product class (FOODON_00002694). No specific peri-peri seasoned fries class exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Potato products (fried) — closest parent in FoodEx2 matrix for seasoned fried potato products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated peri-peri fries entry. A00LC (fried potato products) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for peri-peri fries or seasoned fried potato snacks at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of peri-peri fries is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium-sized side portion of seasoned fried potato strips.

nutrient_source.title: Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, as purchased (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 11400 adapted with peri-peri seasoning adjustment)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for frozen french fries (NDB 11400 / FDC ID 170108) used as the base. Sodium and energy values adjusted upward to reflect peri-peri seasoning blend (salt, chilli, garlic, paprika) typical of restaurant-prepared peri-peri fries. Adjustment methodology follows standard seasoning-addition estimation: sodium increased by approximately 80 mg/100 g to account for spice blend; energy marginally increased by ~5 kcal/100 g for spice coating. All other macros retained from USDA base. This is an adapted estimate; exact restaurant formulation not publicly available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — French fries / fried potato products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 is based on published GI values for french fries / fried potato products from the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Peri-peri seasoning does not materially alter the GI of the potato base. GL per serving calculated as: (75 × 41.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 46.7 g per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from peri-peri seasoning blend and salt added during frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit peri-peri fries due to approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (570 mg per 150 g serving), which represents a significant portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~47) from deep-fried starchy potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit peri-peri fries because the high GI of 75 and glycemic load of approximately 47 per serving will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should be minimised and paired with low-GI foods and adequate protein.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (15 g/100 g) with saturated fat (2.3 g/100 g) and elevated sodium from seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume peri-peri fries only occasionally, as the combination of deep-frying oil, elevated sodium (~380 mg/100 g), and moderate saturated fat content may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peri-Peri Fries are deep-fried potato strips seasoned with a peri-peri spice blend, which typically includes African bird's eye chilli (Capsicum frutescens), garlic, paprika, lemon, herbs, and salt. The dish originates from the Portuguese-African culinary tradition, where peri-peri (also spelled piri-piri) sauce and seasoning became iconic through Portuguese colonial influence in Mozambique and South Africa, later popularised globally by restaurant chains. The fries are characterised by a crispy exterior, fluffy interior, and a bold, spicy, slightly smoky flavour profile. In Singapore, peri-peri fries appear on menus of international casual dining restaurants and sports bars as a side dish or snack. Nutritionally, peri-peri fries are energy-dense at approximately 317 kcal per 100 g, with significant carbohydrate content (41.5 g/100 g) from the potato base and moderate fat (15 g/100 g) from deep-frying. The glycemic index is high (GI ~75), making this food a concern for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Sodium content is elevated (~380 mg/100 g) due to the seasoning blend, which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is generally suitable for vegetarians and vegans but is not recommended for frequent consumption in clinical dietary management of diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in peri-peri fries?
Is peri-peri fries suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of peri-peri fries?
What seasoning is used in peri-peri fries?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for peri-peri fries exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for fried potato products with a documented seasoning adjustment. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (french fries / fried potato). Confidence is medium because the base data is from an approved secondary source with a transparent adaptation methodology.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170108, frozen french fries) with sodium increased by ~80 mg/100 g and energy by ~5 kcal/100 g to account for peri-peri seasoning. This is an estimation; exact restaurant formulation from Dugout - Circular Road is not publicly available. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this fried potato product. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for fried potato products. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this product. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this fried potato product. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food product containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by nature of ingredients. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based fried product with no animal-derived fat; confirmed zero per USDA source. 8. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on published GI values for french fries from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); peri-peri seasoning does not materially alter GI. 9. The input cuisine tag 'Indian' appears to be a field survey platform categorisation artefact; peri-peri fries are a Portuguese-African fusion dish and have been reclassified accordingly. The 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag is also a platform artefact and has been disregarded. 10. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 41.5 × 1.5) / 100 = 46.7.
